did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning techniques, specifically an end-to-end, multi-task interpretable deep learning diagnostic model with a class balancing module, course learning fusion module, and diagnostic decoder, to perform melanoma classification and interpretability analysis on dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, specifically proposing an end-to-end, multi-task interpretable deep learning diagnostic model for melanoma classification. It details the model architecture (class balancing module, course learning fusion module, diagnostic decoder), describes the dataset used (7-point criteria evaluation database with 413 training, 203 validation, and 395 test cases), and reports experimental results including an average accuracy of 74.43%. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICDCOT), it contains original research results rather than being a review or survey.

title: A Multilabel Dermatologic Classification and Interpretability Study for Small Samples and Extremely Unbalanced Classes
journal: 2024 International Conference on Distributed Computing and Optimization Techniques (ICDCOT)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenges of class imbalance and interpretability in melanoma diagnosis by proposing an end-to-end, multi-task interpretable deep learning diagnostic model. The model integrates a class balancing module, a course learning fusion module, and a diagnostic decoder to mimic the clinical 7-point checklist scoring criteria. It was evaluated on the 7-point criteria evaluation database, which was split into 413 training, 203 validation, and 395 test cases. The experimental results demonstrated that the model achieved an average accuracy of 74.43%, outperforming several existing methods. Furthermore, incorporating prior knowledge from the 7-point checklist improved the final melanoma diagnosis accuracy from 70.89% to 72.15%. The findings indicate that the proposed framework effectively enhances both the accuracy and interpretability of deep learning models for dermatological classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary diagnostic target of the study. The model performs binary classification for melanoma (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) using the 7-point checklist criteria (score ≥ 3 indicates melanoma). The dataset is the '7-point criteria evaluation database'.

The negative class in the melanoma diagnosis task. The paper refers to the dataset as containing 'skin disease' and 'dermatologic samples' generally, but the specific diagnostic output is binary for melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Melanoma diagnosis (binary: melanoma vs. non-melanoma) and 7-point checklist label classification (binary for each of the 7 features: Pigment Network, Blue Whitish Veil, Vascular Structures, Streaks, Pigmentation, Dots and Globules, Regression Structures).
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes an end-to-end, multi-task interpretable deep learning diagnostic model for melanoma classification. The model performs binary classification for the final diagnosis (melanoma if score ≥ 3, else non-melanoma) and simultaneously classifies 7 specific dermatological features (labels) from the 7-point checklist. The output is discrete diagnostic categories and label scores (0 or 1), not continuous regression values. The primary task is classification, with an average accuracy of 74.43% reported.

datasets_used.name: 7-point criteria evaluation database
datasets_used.description: A dataset containing dermatoscopic images used for melanoma diagnosis based on the 7-point checklist criteria. The images range in size from 474 × 512 to 532 × 768 pixels. It is cited as reference [20] (Alzahrani et al., 2019).

use.training: Used for training the end-to-end, multi-task interpretable deep learning diagnostic model, including the class balancing module and curriculum learning fusion module.
use.validation: Used for validating the model during the training process.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance and comparing results with other methods (e.g., Alzahrani et al., MmA, EmbeddingNet, TripleNet, Kawahara et al.).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy metrics and compared against other methods in tables without statistical significance testing.

label_classification_and_diagnosis.notes: The proposed model achieved the highest average accuracy (74.4%) among compared methods. The DIAG column represents the diagnosis accuracy based on the 7-point checklist criteria. The AVG column represents the average accuracy across all 7 labels and the final diagnosis.

diagnosis_with_prior_knowledge_fusion.notes: Incorporating prior knowledge from the 7-point checklist improved the final melanoma diagnosis accuracy from 70.89% to 72.15%.

7-point criteria evaluation database.notes: Metrics are reported as percentages. The dataset contains dermatoscopic images ranging from 474 × 512 to 532 × 768 pixels. The 7 labels are: Pigment Network (PN), Blue Whitish Veil (BWV), Vascular Structures (VS), Streaks (STR), Pigmentation (PIG), Dots and Globules (DAG), and Regression Structures (RS). DIAG refers to the final melanoma diagnosis accuracy. AVG refers to the average accuracy across all tasks.

architecture_used.main_model: End-to-end, multi-task interpretable deep learning diagnostic model

architecture_details.backbone: Backbone network (specific architecture not named, referred to as 'backbone network' for feature extraction)
modules: Class balancing module (uses improved Focal Loss (iFL) and dynamic weight adjustment based on categorical share)

Curriculum Learning Fusion (CLF) module (uses attention mechanism to fuse backbone network features and aligned features; gradually increases difficulty of learning by adding label information as training rounds progress)
architecture_details.decoder_structure: Two fully-connected layers; the last fully-connected layer has 7 branches corresponding to different labels
architecture_details.loss_function: Improved Focal Loss (iFL) for class balancing; Cross-entropy loss (Lce) for attention mechanism
architecture_details.optimization: Not explicitly reported (mentions minimizing iFL and using attention weights)

hyperparameters.training_rounds: n (current number of training rounds), m (maximum number of training rounds)

preprocessing: Normalization of brightness, contrast, and color
training_details.learning_strategy: Curriculum learning: focuses on image features in early stages, gradually adds label information as prior knowledge in later stages

outputs: Scores for 7 labels (Pigment Network, Blue Whitish Veil, Vascular Structures, Streaks, Pigmentation, Dots and Globules, Regression Structures)
Final melanoma diagnosis (binary: melanoma if score ≥ 3, else non-melanoma)
classification_or_output_head.structure: 7 branches in the final fully-connected layer for label scoring; final diagnosis derived from summing label scores

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: End-to-end, multi-task interpretable deep learning diagnostic model (Proposed Model)
preprocessing_techniques: Oversampling for minority classes to balance sample distribution
Normalization of brightness, contrast, and color

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Random rotation of different angles, random scaling, and random translation were applied to expand the dataset.
Oversampling technique was used to improve classes with relatively few samples and balance the sample distribution between classes.
Normalization of brightness, contrast, and color was performed to minimize differences in illumination and color across images.

conclusions: The proposed end-to-end, multi-task interpretable deep learning diagnostic model effectively improves the interpretability of deep learning models while maintaining high accuracy in melanoma diagnosis.

The model achieves an average accuracy of 74.43% on the 7-point criteria evaluation database, outperforming existing methods such as Alzahrani et al., MmA, EmbeddingNet, TripleNet, and Kawahara et al.
Incorporating prior knowledge from the 7-point checklist scoring criteria improved the final melanoma diagnosis accuracy from 70.89% to 72.15%.
The class balancing module successfully mitigates the negative impact of data imbalance on model performance in highly unbalanced conditions.
The diagnostic decoder provides transparent results by outputting scores for the 7 specific dermatological features, thereby strengthening doctor trust and providing a basis for decision-making.
The study demonstrates that mimicking the clinical diagnostic process (learning features then combining them with checklist scores) enhances both the credibility and interpretability of the AI model.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small sample size (total 1011 images) and extreme class imbalance, which may affect the generalizability of the results.

The dataset used (7-point criteria evaluation database) is sourced from a single external reference [20] (Alzahrani et al., 2019) without mention of external validation on independent cohorts, raising concerns about dataset bias and overfitting.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was reported to validate the performance improvements over baseline models.

The model's performance (74.43% average accuracy) is modest, and the improvement from prior knowledge fusion (70.89% to 72.15%) is small, suggesting limited clinical impact of the interpretability module on diagnostic accuracy.
The paper does not report the specific backbone architecture used for feature extraction, limiting reproducibility and architectural comparison.

The study was published in a conference proceedings (ICDCOT), which may have less rigorous peer review compared to top-tier journals, and the scope is limited to a specific, potentially non-representative dataset.
